title: Vehicle Accident (Multi-Vehicle)
steps: Ensure your own safety first; do not enter a dangerous traffic zone.
Call emergency services with exact location and number of victims.
If safe, turn off the ignition of involved vehicles to prevent fire.
Apply the parking brake or block wheels of unstable vehicles.
Do NOT move injured persons unless there is an immediate threat of fire or explosion.
Use hazard lights, flares, or warning triangles to alert other drivers.

title: Pedestrian Struck by Vehicle
steps: Ensure traffic is stopped or diverted to protect the victim and yourself.
Call emergency services immediately with the location and victim's condition.
Do NOT move the person unless there is an immediate threat to life (e.g., fire).
Keep the victim's head and neck still to prevent spinal injury.
Check for consciousness and breathing; provide basic first aid (bleeding control) if safe.
Calm the victim and prevent them from trying to stand up until paramedics arrive.

title: Vehicle Fire
steps: Pull over as quickly and safely as possible. Turn off the engine.
Get all passengers out of the vehicle and move at least 100 feet (30 meters) away.
Do NOT open the hood if smoke or flames are coming from it (air will feed the fire).
Call emergency services immediately.
If the fire is small and in the interior, use a fire extinguisher. Do NOT put yourself at risk.
Stay away from the vehicle to avoid toxic fumes or potential explosions.

title: Bicycle Accident
steps: Ensure the cyclist is in a safe location away from moving traffic.
Do NOT remove the helmet unless it is obstructing the airway.
Check for responsiveness and bleeding.
Immobilize the head and neck if you suspect a spinal injury.
If the cyclist was wearing a helmet that took an impact, it must be replaced (it is no longer safe).
Collect witness information and photos of the scene if possible.

title: Public Transit Incident (Train/Bus)
steps: If on a train, stay away from doors and windows during impact.
Be aware of high-voltage 'Third Rails' or overhead lines; do NOT touch the tracks.
Locate emergency exits and hammers (if needed to break glass).
Follow the driver's or conductor's instructions immediately.
If you must evacuate onto tracks, watch for other moving transit vehicles.
Help others evacuate if safe to do so.

title: Submerged Vehicle (Car in Water)
steps: Do NOT use your cell phone until you are out of the vehicle. Every second counts.
UNBUCKLE your seatbelt immediately. Help children unbuckle theirs.
Open or BREAK the side window. Doors may be impossible to open due to water pressure.
Get out through the window. Children should go first.
If you cannot open the window, wait until the car is nearly full of water; the pressure will equalize, allowing the door to open.
Once out, swim to the surface and move away from any currents or the vehicle.

title: Brake Failure
steps: Pump the brake pedal fast and hard. You may be able to build up enough pressure to stop.
Shift into a lower gear (downshift) to let the engine slow the car down.
Use the parking brake (emergency brake) gradually. Do NOT pull it up all the way at once (risk of skidding).
Look for an escape route, such as a grassy area, an open field, or an upgrade.
If you must hit something, look for something soft like bushes or a guardrail.
Use your horn and lights to warn other drivers that your car is out of control.

title: Tire Blowout at High Speed
steps: Grip the steering wheel firmly with both hands to maintain control.
Do NOT slam on the brakes. Hard braking will cause you to lose control of the vehicle.
Gradually ease off the accelerator to let the car slow down on its own.
Correct the steering to stay in your lane; the car will pull to the side of the blowout.
Once you have regained control and slowed down, signal and pull off to a safe spot.
Turn on your hazard lights and call for roadside assistance; do not change the tire on a busy highway.

title: Stuck Throttle / Unintended Acceleration
steps: Shift the vehicle into NEUTRAL (N). This will disconnect the engine from the wheels.
Apply the brakes firmly and steadily. Do NOT pump them (loses vacuum assist).
Steer the vehicle to a safe location off the road.
Once stopped, turn off the engine. Do NOT turn it off while moving (loses power steering/breaks).
Check if the floor mat is interfering with the pedal.
Do not drive the car again until it has been inspected by a professional mechanic.

title: Wrong-Way Driver Response
steps: Slow down and move as far to the right as possible immediately.
Flash your headlights and honk your horn to get the other driver's attention.
Do NOT pull onto the left shoulder; that is where the wrong-way driver is most likely to go.
If a collision is imminent, aim for the 'least dangerous' object (bush, fence) rather than the vehicle.
Once the vehicle has passed, safely pull over and call 112/911 to report the incident.
Stay alert for other drivers who may be swerving to avoid the wrong-way vehicle.

title: Stuck in Traffic / Gridlock Emergency
steps: If you are stuck for a long period in extreme heat, turn off the engine to prevent overheating, but stay inside if it is safer.
Stay hydrated. Keep emergency water in your car for these situations.
Keep your doors locked and windows up if the area is unsafe or congestion is due to civil unrest.
Monitor local radio or GPS apps for the cause of the delay and estimated clearance time.
Do NOT use the shoulder for passing unless instructed by emergency personnel; the shoulder is for emergency vehicles only.
If you have a medical emergency while stuck, call 112/911 and provide your exact coordinates and car description.

title: Wildlife on Road (Collision Avoidance)
steps: If an animal (like a deer) jumps in front of you, BRAKE firmly and stay in your lane. Do NOT swerve.
Swerving to avoid an animal can lead to a much more dangerous head-on collision or rollover.
Use your high beams at night in wildlife areas to spot eyes reflected in the distance.
If you hit a large animal, pull over safely and stay away from it; injured animals can be very dangerous.
Report the collision to local authorities, especially if the animal is obstructing the roadway.
Check your vehicle for fluid leaks or damage before attempting to drive again.
